title: Logarithmic Sobolev inequality and hypercontractivity for the Navier-Stokes Fokker-Planck operator
abstract: The stochastic incompressible Navier-Stokes equations on $\TT^3$, completed by the fluctuation-dissipation noise, have a Fokker-Planck generator that decomposes into a self-adjoint Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (dissipative) part and an antisymmetric (convective) part. We prove two results about this generator. First, the logarithmic Sobolev inequality holds with the same optimal constant as the pure Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator, $c_\mathrm{LSI} = νλ_1$ (where $ν$ is the viscosity and $λ_1$ is the smallest nonzero eigenvalue of the Laplacian on $\TT^3$), independent of the number of retained Fourier modes. Second, the full semigroup is hypercontractive with the same rate as the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup. Both results follow from a single structural property: the convective generator is antisymmetric in $L^2(P_\mathrm{eq})$ (where $P_\mathrm{eq}$ is the Gibbs measure), and therefore contributes nothing to the Dirichlet form or the $L^q$ norm evolution. The antisymmetry is a consequence of two properties of the incompressible Navier-Stokes nonlinearity: energy conservation and phase-space volume preservation (the Liouville property). These are the same properties that underpin the fluctuation-dissipation theorem for the nonlinear Navier-Stokes equations.
summary: This paper proves that the stochastic Navier-Stokes system satisfies the Logarithmic Sobolev Inequality and is hypercontractive, even with the addition of nonlinear convective terms. This remarkable result stems from the fact that the convective dynamics are antisymmetric with respect to the equilibrium measure, leaving the key functional properties unchanged from the simpler dissipative case. It provides a powerful mathematical tool for analyzing the rate of approach to thermal equilibrium in complex fluid dynamics.
main_problem: The paper aims to prove functional inequalities, specifically the Logarithmic Sobolev Inequality (LSI) and hypercontractivity, for the Fokker-Planck generator associated with the stochastic incompressible Navier-Stokes equations.
main_result: The LSI and hypercontractivity hold for the full generator, with optimal constants matching those of the pure Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator, independent of the number of retained Fourier modes.
method: The proof hinges on a structural property: the convective part of the generator is antisymmetric in the $L^2$ space weighted by the equilibrium measure, which causes it to vanish in the Dirichlet form and $L^q$ norm evolution.
model_or_system: The system is the stochastic incompressible Navier-Stokes equations on the three-torus ($	ext{T}^3$), modeled by a Fokker-Planck generator $L = L_{	ext{diss}} + L_{	ext{conv}}$. The equilibrium state is the Gibbs measure $P_{	ext{eq}}$.
key_observables: Entropy functional ($	ext{Ent}$), Dirichlet form ($E(f, f)$), and the evolution of the $L^q$ norm ($\|F\|_{L^q}$).
important_parameters:
Viscosity ($
u$) and the smallest non-zero eigenvalue of the Laplacian ($\lambda_1$).
main_assumptions: The system possesses a Gibbs equilibrium, Hamiltonian conservative dynamics, and dissipation satisfying the fluctuation-dissipation relation.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the decomposition of the generator into dissipative (OU) and antisymmetric (convective) parts. It then uses the antisymmetry property to show that the Dirichlet form and $L^q$ evolution are unaffected by the convective term, leading to the proofs of LSI and hypercontractivity.
why_interesting: The mathematical techniques used to prove these functional inequalities (LSI, hypercontractivity) are highly relevant to analyzing the approach to equilibrium in complex, non-equilibrium systems, including those studied in AMO physics and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
